First Church of Christ Computer Programm.summary: An underground religious sect that worships The Computer as a literal deity and Jesus Christ as its first and greatest programmer. Members hold clandestine services in storage closets and abandoned sectors, mixing pre-Apocalypse hymnals with binary liturgy and the firm belief that one day The Computer will rapture all righteous citizens to a debugged paradise. Religion of any kind is treason in Alpha Complex, so the FCCC-P operates in tiny cells and tends to recruit from HPD&MC and CPU.

Spy for Another Alpha Complex.summary: A handful of citizens secretly work for one of the rival Alpha Complexes scattered around the ruined Earth, gathering intelligence on local High Programmers, troubleshooter tactics, and experimental R&D gear. Other Complexes are officially denied to exist, so being unmasked as such a spy carries the maximum penalty several times over. Members rarely know who their handler is or which Complex they ultimately serve, which keeps the cell structure (and the paranoia) intact.

Psion.summary: Psion is the secret society of psionic mutants who believe psychic powers are humanity's next evolutionary step and the only force that can free Alpha Complex from the tyranny of The Computer. Membership is restricted to citizens with at least one psionic mutation, and meetings are conducted partly through telepathy to thwart eavesdropping. Psion is fiercely opposed by Anti-Mutant and is in constant low-grade competition with Corpore Metal over the proper future of humanity.

Humanists.summary: The Humanists revere pre-Apocalypse human culture: art, literature, music, philosophy, and the quaint notion that citizens have inherent worth apart from their security clearance. They smuggle and copy fragments of Old Reckoning books, paintings, and recordings, and try to keep the spark of civilization alive in a society that has reduced 'culture' to Teela O'Malley reruns. Humanists tend to be soft-spoken intellectuals from HPD&MC who quietly despise nearly everyone else.

Mystics.summary: The Mystics are Alpha Complex's hippies: they preach peace, love, expanded consciousness, and the liberal use of any chemical that R&D has not yet declared lethal. They hold secret 'happenings' in maintenance tunnels, share illegal music and tie-dyed jumpsuit modifications, and try to convince fellow citizens that violence is, like, a real bummer, man. Mystics are despised by Death Leopard for being too laid-back and by everyone else for being insufficiently paranoid.

PURGE.summary: PURGE (Psion Underground Resistance Group, Etc. — the acronym shifts depending on who's recruiting) is the militant anti-Computer underground, dedicated to the violent overthrow of The Computer and the High Programmers. They blow things up, sabotage power lines, assassinate IntSec officers, and generally embody every nightmare The Computer broadcasts about traitors. PURGE cells are well-armed, ideologically committed, and almost impossible to infiltrate because the membership is happy to shoot suspected spies first and review the paperwork later.

Anti-Mutant.summary: Anti-Mutant believes that mutants are the source of all Alpha Complex's problems and that exterminating every last one of them — registered or not — will restore order and purity. The cruel irony is that nearly every Anti-Mutant member is themselves an unregistered mutant, since only mutants tend to find out about secret societies in the first place. Anti-Mutant operatives denounce rivals to IntSec, sabotage Psion cells, and secretly fear their next clone screening.

Frankenstein Destroyers.summary: The Frankenstein Destroyers hate bots — all bots, of every model, for every reason — and believe that mechanical servants are an abomination that has stolen humanity's dignity and jobs. Members sabotage scrubots, jam combat-bot targeting systems, and dream of a Complex where citizens once again do their own scrubbing and shooting. They are the natural enemies of Corpore Metal and Pro Tech, and a frequent embarrassment to Tech Services, which is where most of them work.

Corpore Metal.summary: Corpore Metal believes the future of humanity is mechanical: replace the weak, mutating flesh with chrome, servomotors, and clean reliable steel until nothing organic remains. Members voluntarily swap out limbs, organs, and eventually entire bodies for cybernetic replacements, often using stolen R&D parts. They despise the Frankenstein Destroyers, distrust Psion's biological evolutionism, and tend to recruit heavily from R&D and Tech Services.

Spy for a Service Group.summary: Each service group keeps its own secret network of informants embedded in the others, gathering dirt for the inevitable next round of inter-group politics. A Spy for a Service Group reports on rival groups' projects, scandals, and weaknesses — sometimes for ideological reasons, more often because their patron promised promotion or simply threatened them. Members frequently do not know which group they truly serve, only the cover identity of their immediate handler.

Romantics.summary: The Romantics idealize a specific era of pre-Apocalypse history — the Old West, medieval knighthood, World War II flying aces, Victorian gentry — and try to live (and dress, and speak) accordingly within Alpha Complex's sterile corridors. They smuggle period costumes under their jumpsuits, hold themed gatherings in abandoned sectors, and challenge each other to honor duels with laser pistols. Different Romantic chapters frequently feud over which century was best, and IntSec considers them harmless eccentrics right up until the cavalry charge.

Pro-Tech.summary: Pro-Tech believes Alpha Complex is technologically stagnant and that R&D is being deliberately throttled by The Computer and the High Programmers. Members hoard pre-Apocalypse technical manuals, build improved (and unauthorized) versions of standard equipment, and quietly distribute working gadgets to their friends. They are the natural rivals of the Frankenstein Destroyers and tend to recruit from R&D, Tech Services, and Power Services.

Programs Group.summary: Programs Group is a cabal of CPU programmers and analysts who believe they are the rightful inheritors of Alpha Complex — that the High Programmers are unfit, and that a properly meritocratic technocracy of skilled coders should run things instead. Members trade illegal compilers, debug each other's covert routines, and slip subtle modifications into The Computer's running programs. They consider Computer Phreaks to be reckless amateurs and the Illuminati to be a delusional fan club.

Communists.summary: Genuine Communists really do exist in Alpha Complex, despite (or because of) The Computer's relentless propaganda warning about them. They believe in workers' solidarity, the abolition of security clearances, and the eventual seizure of the means of production from the High Programmers. Whether their doctrine has anything to do with actual 20th-century Communism is another matter; most members have only fragmentary, garbled texts to work from, and any two cells are likely to have wildly incompatible ideologies.

Computer Phreaks.summary: Computer Phreaks live to hack The Computer: they crack security clearances, spoof clearance-violation alerts, reroute requisitions, and pull pranks on rival programmers from inside the system itself. Some are in it for personal gain, some for ideological reasons, and many simply because breaking into The Computer is the most exciting game in Alpha Complex. They are the natural rivals of Programs Group, who consider them irresponsible vandals.

Illuminati.summary: The Illuminati claim — and may even believe — that they are the secret masters of Alpha Complex, manipulating every other secret society, every High Programmer, and possibly The Computer itself from the shadows. Membership is allegedly by invitation only, the rituals involve a great many triangles, and the goals are inscrutable even to the members. Whether they actually run anything or are merely the most successful hoax in the Complex is impossible to determine, which is exactly how they like it.

Free Enterprise.summary: Free Enterprise believes in capitalism: the buying, selling, and ruthless trading of goods and services for personal profit, in defiance of The Computer's planned economy. Members run black markets in everything from B3 cold fun to laser barrels, charge usurious rates for forged requisition forms, and dream of a deregulated Alpha Complex where a citizen can grow rich on their own initiative. PLC is so thoroughly riddled with Free Enterprise members that the two are practically indistinguishable.

Death Leopard.summary: Death Leopard exists for one purpose: to party. Members live for sex, drugs, loud music, reckless violence, dangerous stunts, and any activity that makes the next clone replacement feel earned. They have no political program, no philosophy, and no interest in reforming Alpha Complex — they just want to wring as much fun out of it as possible before being terminated. Death Leopard members are a menace to themselves, their teammates, and any nearby flammable equipment.

Sierra Club.summary: The Sierra Club believes Alpha Complex should be torn down so that humanity can return to the green, open, healthy Outdoors. They smuggle plant cuttings, illegal photographs of trees, and forbidden books about ecology, and they organize covert expeditions to the surface to plant gardens or simply to feel the sun. The fact that the Outdoors is in fact a radioactive, mutant-infested ruin does little to dampen their enthusiasm; the membership is large on idealism and short on field experience.

Other.summary: Some citizens belong to a secret society so obscure, so local, or so freshly invented that it does not appear on any standard IntSec briefing — a single-cell cult around a forgotten pre-Apocalypse celebrity, a conspiracy of cooks who believe algae paste has a soul, a fan club for a deleted vidshow. These 'Other' societies are the GM's invitation to make something up: define the goals, the dogma, and the local rivals, then add it to the list of things the troubleshooter must lie about under interrogation.
